prompt:
Storage-infra ticket. Every time we add or drain a node in the shard fleet, somebody hand-writes the migration list in a spreadsheet, and last month's copy-paste moved the same replica twice. I want a planner that computes the migration list for us. To be clear about scope: this is NOT a consistent-hash ring for per-key lookup — it takes the whole current assignment table and emits an explicit bulk migration plan. Module go-shardplan, package shardplan, stdlib only.

API:
- type Move struct { Shard string; Replica int; From, To string } — From "" means the slot was previously unassigned (new shard or replica growth), To "" means the slot is being dropped (replica shrink).
- type Plan struct { Target map[string][]string; Moves []Move } — Target maps every shard to exactly `replicas` node names, slot i = the node holding replica i. Moves sorted by shard name, then replica index.
- func Rebalance(current map[string][]string, nodes []string, replicas int) (*Plan, error)
- func Check(assign map[string][]string, nodes []string, replicas int) error — the invariant gate ops runs after applying a plan: every shard has exactly `replicas` slots, every slot names a known node, no node appears twice within one shard (replica anti-affinity), and node load spread max-min <= 1. nil when clean; the error message must name an offending shard or node.
- func (p *Plan) Diff() []string — one line per move, same order: `x/0: a -> c` (relocation), `y/1: + b` (new placement), `z/2: - d` (dropped slot).

Rebalance semantics. Inputs: current (may reference nodes that have since left, may have shorter or longer slot lists than `replicas`, may even hold duplicate nodes from a bad hand edit — all of that gets repaired), nodes = the node set to plan FOR, replicas = replicas per shard. The shard set is exactly the keys of current (an empty slot list means a brand-new shard). Validation errors: replicas < 1, replicas > len(nodes), empty node name, duplicate node names. The order of the nodes argument must never matter.

I am prescribing the algorithm's tie-breaks because two SREs on two laptops must produce byte-identical plans:
1. Keep what's valid. Walk shards by name ascending, slots by index ascending: a slot keeps its node when that node is in the plan's node set AND the same shard doesn't already keep that node at a lower index. Slots at index >= replicas are dropped (a Move with To ""). Every other slot becomes a hole remembering its old entry as Move.From ("" when the slot never existed).
2. Per-node targets. total = shards*replicas, base = total/len(nodes): rank nodes by kept-load descending, name ascending as the tie-break; the first total%len(nodes) nodes get base+1, the rest get base. (Loaded nodes keep their surplus — fewer moves.)
3. Evict overflow. Visit nodes in name-ascending order; while a node keeps more slots than its target, evict one kept slot, which becomes a hole with From = that node. Choosing the victim: prefer slots whose shard has NO hole pending yet in this run (we apply plans replica-by-replica, and a shard losing two replicas in one plan means a window where it serves nothing — avoid that whenever possible); among the preferred (or among all of them when every shard on the node is already holed), take the smallest (shard name, replica index).
4. Fill holes in (shard name, replica index) ascending order. Candidates: nodes still below their target that do NOT already hold that shard. Pick the lowest current load; break ties by name ascending. If no candidate exists, fail with an error exactly of the form `cannot place shard "m" replica 1` — loud beats lopsided.
Emit one Move per changed slot. Re-planning an assignment that is already clean returns an empty (but non-nil) Moves list, and re-planning a plan's own Target is a no-op — idempotence is what makes apply retries safe.

Acceptance contract: shardplan_test.go; `go test -race -timeout 120s ./...` must pass without touching the test file.
